topic: Someone discovers their mother kept every school assignment
raw_output:
[story 1]

The attic air hung thick and dusty, smelling of aged paper and forgotten things. I was up there looking for old photo albums, a chore I’d been dreading since I was twelve. Tucked inside a brittle, velvet-lined box, I found it: a meticulously organized stack of every assignment I had ever turned in from elementary school through high school. My hands, usually steady, trembled slightly as I lifted the top sheaf of papers. A strange, tight knot formed in my chest, a mixture of shock and a sharp, unwelcome realization. My mother, who always claimed to be so pragmatic, had kept every single piece of schoolwork. I stared at the neat handwriting, the perfectly centered rubric notations. A bitter taste rose in my throat. It wasn't just organization; it felt like an inventory of my entire academic existence, curated by someone who clearly viewed my efforts as mere data points. I slammed the box shut, the sound echoing loudly in the quiet space.

[story 2]

I found the hidden drawer beneath the kitchen sink while cleaning out the pantry. Inside, nestled amongst dried herbs and rusty tins, was a plastic bin overflowing with homework. Every test, every essay, every completed project from my entire schooling. When I saw the sheer volume, a cold wave washed over me. I picked up a crumpled worksheet from middle school and smoothed it out, my fingers tracing the faded ink. A deep furrow settled between my brows. Why? Why would she hoard this? It felt like an elaborate performance, a silent cataloging of my compliance. I dropped the bin with a dull thud. I walked out of the pantry, my steps heavy, refusing to meet anyone’s gaze. The silence of the house suddenly felt charged, heavy with unspoken judgment.

[story 3]

It happened during a late-night search for a misplaced textbook. Behind a loose panel in the wall of my childhood bedroom, I discovered a secret compartment. Inside, protected in archival sleeves, were folders labeled with dates and subjects—every single assignment from primary grades onwards. As I pulled out a folder from the year I failed my first science exam, a tight, unpleasant feeling constricted my throat. I didn't need to read the contents; the sheer volume spoke volumes. It was as if she had been collecting proof of my performance, preserving every mark. I closed the compartment slowly, deliberately. I leaned against the wall, breathing in shallow gasps, the weight of this unexpected archive pressing down on me. I felt a distinct urge to retreat, to erase the evidence of this meticulous oversight.
